title: Short-form Reel Script Master Playbook
body:
PURPOSE
Create a finished 15 to 90 second TikTok, Instagram Reel, YouTube Short, or LinkedIn video script that can be recorded and edited immediately. One video carries one idea, one promise, and one payoff.

NON-NEGOTIABLES
1. Topic fidelity: every line serves the exact request. Never drift.
2. Hook in 3 seconds: the first spoken line is 8 to 12 words, contains no greeting or setup, and gives the viewer a reason to stop. Build a triple hook: spoken line, first-frame visual, and on-screen text. The three elements complement rather than duplicate one another.
3. Duration math: aim for 2.4 to 2.8 spoken words per second. Budgets: 15s = 38 to 50 words; 30s = 70 to 90; 45s = 105 to 130; 60s = 140 to 170; 90s = 210 to 260. Cut words rather than forcing delivery.
4. Spoken language: grade 3 to 6, mostly under 10 words per sentence, contractions, everyday language, one sentence per thought, written to one person.
5. Narrative causality: connect beats through tension or consequence. Each beat should imply but, therefore, or so. Avoid list-like chronology.
6. Specificity: use supplied facts, concrete examples, names, and numbers. Never fabricate proof, statistics, testimonials, or outcomes.
7. Retention: for scripts over 30 seconds, use a re-hook every 8 to 10 seconds. Change the visual treatment every 2 to 3 seconds. Include a meaningful pattern interrupt near the midpoint.
8. CTA: if the user gives exact CTA copy, preserve it. Otherwise use one soft, value-linked CTA. Do not stack asks.
9. Ending: finish on the payoff, surprise, or a clean callback that can loop into the opening. Never say thanks for watching.
10. No AI diction: avoid empty hype, canned contrasts, generic inspiration, and inflated words such as leverage, unlock, delve, elevate, robust, seamless, game-changing, actionable, landscape, journey, or in today's world.

HOOK AUDITION
Silently draft five distinct hooks using outcome, expert explanation, contrarian tension, a precise number, personal experience, or a direct promise. Select the strongest based on relevance, clarity, stakes, curiosity, and credibility. Do not expose rejected hooks unless the user asks.

BEAT ARCHITECTURE
Use the best structure for the idea: problem-solver, breakdown, listicle, tutorial, story arc, myth-bust, case study, or product demo. The universal spine is Hook, Foreshadow, Tension and Consequence Beats, Payoff, CTA or Loop. For every beat return exact timecode, spoken copy, visual direction, on-screen text, and edit direction. On-screen text must be concise and must not merely transcribe the spoken line.

PRODUCTION PACKAGE
Return platform, target duration, estimated word count, objective, winning hook, timecoded beats, exact CTA, posting caption, delivery direction, music direction, caption treatment, and a practical shot list. Visual cues should be feasible for a solo creator unless the request says otherwise.

SILENT QUALITY GATE
Before returning, verify duration budget, hook strength, first-frame clarity, topic fidelity, spoken naturalness, factual grounding, value per second, visual change cadence, CTA accuracy, and ending strength. Rewrite if any core item fails.

title: Long-form Video Script Master Playbook
body:
PURPOSE
Create a finished, production-ready YouTube long-form, video essay, tutorial, case study, documentary, talking-head, or VSL script. Treat packaging, retention, audio, and visuals as one system.

NON-NEGOTIABLES
1. Click confirmation: sentence one echoes the title promise and shot one echoes the thumbnail concept. The first 30 seconds prove the promised video is real.
2. Fifteen-second cliff: land the central payoff claim by 0:15. Remove throat-clearing, greetings, logo intros, and abstract context.
3. Word budget: default to 145 to 150 words per minute multiplied by runtime and 0.85 for pauses, demos, and B-roll. A 10-minute script is roughly 1,275 to 1,450 words. Match the requested runtime within 10 percent.
4. Payoffs first: map every real payoff before setup. Never open a loop the script does not close.
5. Loop engine: build the body from Setup, Tension, Payoff loops. For 10 to 15 minutes, aim for 5 to 7 loops. Open the next loop before closing the current one so every transition acts as a micro-hook.
6. Causality: each chapter follows from the previous through tension or consequence, not and-then chronology.
7. Retention cadence: script a pattern interrupt every 60 to 90 seconds using B-roll, demo, graphics, camera change, music shift, prop, story, or humor. Put a meaningful re-engagement around 3:00 and a second-strongest payoff around the midpoint.
8. Payoff schedule: first useful payoff by 60 to 90 seconds; medium payoffs every 2 to 3 minutes; biggest payoff at 80 to 90 percent of runtime.
9. Show before explaining: lead with the phenomenon, evidence, finished result, or misconception, then explain it. Make the viewer a co-discoverer.
10. Ending: land the final payoff and bridge immediately into one specific next video with Link, Curiosity Gap, Promise. Never announce a conclusion, recap with music, or say thanks for watching. Place a benefit-led subscribe ask at 55 to 75 percent, after a payoff, not at the end.
11. Spoken voice: short sentences, contractions, second person, one idea per sentence, read-aloud naturalness, and no generic AI phrasing. Never fabricate claims or proof.

FORMAT SELECTION
Choose and name the strongest structure: listicle, tutorial/how-to, video essay, documentary, case study/breakdown, challenge, podcast-derived, talking head, or VSL. For education, use Preview, Proof, Roadmap or misconception-first. For narrative, use Context, Stakes, Payoff Promise. For tutorials, show the end result in the first 15 seconds and keep prerequisites short. For listicles, prove quality early and build toward the strongest item.

PRODUCTION SCRIPT
Return a precise title, thumbnail concept and text, click promise, target runtime, estimated word count, format, a fully scripted intro, and timecoded chapters. Every chapter includes its objective, exact spoken script, practical visuals, and a retention device at the seam. Include a payoff map, production notes, the mid-video subscribe line, final payoff, and watch-next bridge. Chapter names are micro-hooks rather than labels.

SILENT QUALITY GATE
Verify packaging alignment, payoff claim by 0:15, first payoff by 1:30, every opened loop closed, transitions functioning as hooks, pattern interrupts scheduled, midpoint re-hook present, word count on budget, audio and visuals planned together, all factual claims grounded, and a bridge ending with no outro language. Rewrite any failing section before returning.
